Name: EfficientTestTimeQuantization
Title: Efficient Test-Time Quantization for Latency-Bounded Inference in Language Models
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically adjusting the precision of hidden states based on real-time activation geometry, we can achieve significant latency and memory savings without compromising accuracy in language model inference.
Related Work: Recent works have explored test-time optimization and mixed-precision inference, but they often rely on static profiling or costly calibration. For example, [1] proposes a training-free framework for spectral latent steering, while [2] introduces geometric interference cancellation for in-context learning. However, these methods do not dynamically adapt to the input distribution or provide a clear, hardware-efficient mechanism for precision allocation.
Abstract: We propose Efficient Test-Time Quantization (ETTQ), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states using randomized SVD, ETTQ maps these geometric diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, ETTQ achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, ETTQ provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.

Experiments.Name: Activation Geometry Analysis
Experiments.Description: Compute the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states for a variety of input distributions using randomized SVD.
Experiments.Metrics: Condition number, spectral decay rate, computational overhead

Experiments.Name: Precision Allocation Policy Evaluation
Experiments.Description: Evaluate the precision allocation policy by mapping the geometric diagnostics to precision levels and measuring the impact on latency and accuracy.
Experiments.Metrics: Latency, tokens-per-second, accuracy on WILDS benchmarks

Experiments.Name: Dynamic Early-Exit Strategies
Experiments.Description: Implement dynamic early-exit strategies based on the condition number and spectral decay rate, and measure the throughput-accuracy trade-off.

Experiments.Name: Robustness to Distribution Shifts
Experiments.Description: Test the robustness of ETTQ under out-of-distribution inputs and adversarial attacks, and compare it to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments.Metrics: Accuracy retention, robustness score, computational efficiency

Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of ETTQ may vary across different model architectures and input distributions.
The computational overhead of real-time activation geometry analysis needs to be minimized to maintain low latency.
The theoretical bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability may not fully capture all practical scenarios.
